Hey, it's Andre and Sean. Today is week three of the Art of the Email workshop. The topics for this week are supposed to be value newsletter, bridge emails, and story power promotions. We're going to cover those three topics and we're going to do a little preview for the PLS that module is released today, actually. So we'll be talking about it next week. But we have a PLS coming up, so we thought we'd just take this opportunity and we'd give you kind of a preview of what it's like to be thinking about a product launch series almost a month before it happens. And we haven't had this conversation yet, actually. So I'm going to get kind of a window into our, into some of the really initial thinking that goes into a PLS. But before we talk about that, let's jump up and start talking about a value newsletter. And what I've done is I've just, we're looking at a list of questions and we're just going to go through them and see where they lead. So the first question really is, very broadly speaking, how do we think about and how should our listeners think about creating a value newsletter? I'll kick this off with just a comment from a conversation I had with a really good friend on Friday. Using the example, if anyone listening to this is familiar with Tim Ferriss's Five Bullet Friday email newsletter. And, you know, overall, I've subscribed to it for years. The person I was talking to, his name is Aaron, subscribed to it for years too. But the comment that he made is, we kind of joked around about is that when he gets this newsletter, he doesn't really know what he's supposed to do with it. Sometimes there's a link to a documentary or there's a quote or there's a podcast episode. And it's, it's, it ends up happening in the joke that I made. When we are talking about is oftentimes when I get through the Five Bullet Friday email and there's nothing that I need to do, I feel a sense of relief. I'm like, oh, good. Like, I don't, I don't have to figure this out. And, you know, I really appreciate everything that Tim Ferriss is doing. I've listened to more Tim Ferriss interviews, like any other podcaster. And I think what he's doing is incredibly valuable. But it is interesting that a newsletter, it creates a newsletter creates a cognitive response and an emotional response. And we, and being aware of that, it's really important. Like what impact does this have on the reader? Do they know what to do? Is it too much, too little? Is it easy to ignore because there's really not much there? Is it easy to ignore because there's way too much there? And I don't know where that Goldilocks sweet spot is. So how do you, how do you think about this, Andre? How do you think about like finding that, that middle ground where it's the value newsletters? It's doing what it's supposed to do, but it's not doing too much or too little, too frequent, too infrequent. I'm just curious to hear your thoughts on this. Yeah, I mean, there's never going to be a one-size-fits-all. I think this is more about, you know, it's about aligning with how you want to show up in the world. And this is the, you know, our value newsletter is a reflection of stuff that interests us and the level of depth that we enjoy writing about. But, you know, it takes us a significant amount of time to write our newsletters for the most part because they're quite, you know, it requires us to think on the page and then clarify our thinking. And, you know, that's a resource that we're willing to put in because it lights us up. But it's not for everybody. Other people, they just love researching and just consuming stuff. And for them, they've perhaps curated a certain type of audience that they enjoy those sorts of things. So that person becomes like a conduit, like a middleman that's done the work of filtering through the noise and trying to find a little signal. And then that gets put into a value newsletter. You know, Tim Ferriss has done it. You know, his framing is a five bullet Friday. You know, some people we receive some of the newsletters and it's not a five bullet Friday. It's a 500 bullet Friday. It's it can get ridiculous, but it's just, you know, and then there's Harvard versions and we've we've we've cited a few in the module ones that we enjoy. But, you know, even the ones that we enjoy, I mean, for instance, we've, you know, we've been working really hard over these last few weeks, delivering this this this course as we as we shooting this this recording. And, you know, we're any what halfway through this now. And one of the one of the newsletters that I really enjoy is from Carl Westaway. He's he's the he's the lawyer attorney. And but in my inbox, I think I've got five of his emails, he sends them every Saturday. So I've got five weeks worth of his email sitting in my inbox because I don't want to. I don't have the time to read them just yet, but I don't want to click on them because I'm I'm using email client that when you click on it and look at it, it then disappears. These these different layers of of of stuff we do to each other. But anyway, so I'll ask the one to read that. But it's going to get to a point when there's maybe seven of those things stacked up and I'm just I don't have the bandwidth. So I'm going to just, you know, mark them as red and then just draw a line under it that I'm going to start to read them again when they start to come in again. But then there's other emails that are just easy to read. You can just I can just quickly. So this said is far with them that I haven't seen a problem. I'll just quickly go through them. Dense discovery is is is one of those that come out every Tuesday that we've cited in the module. And yeah, you know, those I can they short I can get through them really easily. And that's and it's always valuable. So I think there's there's just that balance and it's never going to be a be a be a perfect fit. Is there. And it's, you know, we see this question occasionally. It's some variation of it, too. It's, you know, some variation of well, so and so says I should send whatever. It's usually usually the quiz. It's, you know, Ben Settle says I should send emails every day. You know, should I do a daily newsletter? And it's like, well, who knows, right? I mean, it really depends on the first of all, how much time do you have? How much does your audience want to hear from you? How much are they here? It's there's a there's no right answer to that question. And really the way I think to think about it is really you have to start by defining what is valuable to your audience related to your area of expertise or interest or experience, whatever it is. And then like, what's the nature of that value? Is that something somebody wants to like Ben Settle as an example? His audience, like I think his I don't read his emails, but I have read his emails. But I think his audience is reading that email every day because it's part of their professional life for many of them. And it's, you know, it can be inspiration. It can be interesting. Lots of things makes perfect sense for it in that scenario. But if your audience isn't engaging with you every single day, if that doesn't make sense for them, then that format wouldn't make sense. So there is no prescriptive answer here. It really is taking the time to take a step back and ask yourself, how does what I do fit in with what my audience wants and gets value from? And then what what what frequency and what depth make sense? Is it a lot of shallow things, one deep thing, lots of deep things? And then once you kind of get an idea for that, it's just a matter of experimenting with it. We've experimented. I think before mile rively, you did, you know, semi regular, but I don't think you had a very specific schedule for at least one period of time. And then we've, you know, we switched over to bi-weekly, fortnightly. And then we went to weekly and I suspect we were we were sending newsletters weekly over the summer. So it's hard to tease out whether it was summer or an increased frequency. But I suspect the weekly, the combination of weekly emails with a lot more in-depth content, probably at least some percentage of our audience was too much. Right. And we've since gone back to bi-weekly. So who knows? And there's no, there's no right answer here. I think what's what's just to continue on with the VNL, let's talk about theme series versus one-offs. Like when if I'm a if I'm an art of email customer and I'm thinking to myself, well, how when do I when do I just send a newsletter versus when do I put a two or more together into a theme series? And we're going to talk about a use case for that later. But just in general, how do you think about the theme series versus the one-off? Yeah, I mean, we can from our perspective is is we we have an idea, I think of topics we would like to cover over the course of a year. And within the context of that year, there will be X number of of product launch series that we that we're planning to to put out, you know, because of this open and closed enrollment, like a momentum building workshop. So, you know, that all factors in. So I think one of the things that we think about is so long as we are we are covering all the topics that we know that our audience care about. And we want to share there's going to be certain times where theming, theming something is going to make more sense. Or if a certain topic needs to be covered at a certain depth that can't be done in a single VSL VNL. So, you know, most of our emails are quite dense anyway, you know, between a thousand and two thousand words. And if there's something that we want to say that's even deeper than that, well, you know, sending out a five thousand word email is probably not going to solve the problem. It's there's going to be less value on that because it's just going to be too cognitively dense. So we will then break that out over a number of of of news. And if we coming up towards a promotion that makes sense that there's certain things we want to cover, we can theme that. We will mention our Black Friday thing coming up. There's we have some ideas for that. And there's a certain theme that that that makes sense that we talk about, you know, even for ourselves, you know, just to get into that mindset of. Of deep diving into that thing for us to think about that suddenly to switch gears and think more deeply about that subject. It, you know, it's nice when we're writing about it because then it's just flicking us into that mode. And then it's, you know, we transition straight into a promotion and it just it just flows nicely and it's gives people, you know, creates some level of tension in some cases. If that's intentional for us. Do you have any ideas on anymore on theming. Yeah, I mean, I think, you know, theme is just it's good thing to have in the toolbox and I think you've covered the reasons to do it. I mean, as much as I suspect we're going to do a 5003 email and we get to the end of the year, which we'll talk about in a second. Yeah, in general, we don't want people to read chapter from war and peace in one of our emails sort of break something out over weeks, but also to break something out of weeks to pull attention forward. In this summer is we're recording this in 2021. In the summer of 2021 we did a four part series called courage and perseverance over four weeks, and there was there's a lot in that series. It's not way too much for a single, you know, it could be a mini course effectively, but it was pulled attention from each of those emails from beginning to end through the entirety of it. So, and in that sense, you know, that could be that that series could be almost converted into a soap opera series is there's it has all of the ingredients of serialized narrative in it to maintain attention. There's so many reasons to do this and sometimes it's just, you know, the one off just makes sense. That's a good segue into the next thing on the list here which is, you know, sometimes there's just there's there's content you want to create as an idea like you know, when it's jumping into my mind also from the summer was a simple testing framework like that. That came out of a segment from Sesame Street right but it was one of the things when you hear it like that really, like, we get that really maps well to a very complicated thing that our audience could understand in a way that's much easier so let's just, let's create that and it's like it's a gift to the audience like here. Here's a way to take something complicated and make it simpler and it 1500 words 2000 words whatever it was. And that that segues well into this next idea which is sometimes the content that we're creating we don't want it to just disappear. It was in a newsletter, or as an email a year ago or something that we want to repurpose that content to to further expand the world that we're building for our audience creates entry, you know, organic entry points into our world. It creates externally facing value for our audience whether or not they're paying customers. They can be new new entries into our world or somebody's been with us for a long time. But by taking the content that we're creating for a newsletter, changing it slightly to remove the specific references to the email or any of the things we prefaced. And then putting that on our website and saying hey this is part this is the type of thing that we create with our emerging marketing series if you're interested in more like this, then you can sign up so it becomes effectively meaning we don't like to use the term lead magnet because we're not offering behind a an opt-in wall or putting it right out front and saying hey if you like this, we do lots of stuff like this and sign up for our email and it just it's, it's not it's very little extra work to have to do that so I think theme and world building like those they seem to be related in many ways, although now that I'm thinking about most of what no we have we have some multi part series on the site now. I think chefs and cooks was over two parts. So the theme can work for world building or not. So those two things are related. Anything on topic of world building. You want to add for the value newsletter. We did a series for the for the general business that they played out over five emails, I think it was and that's that's been repurposed onto the website. That's linked to in the lesson, I think, actually, one of the things that's worth drawing attention to that we that we haven't explicitly said laid out in in the course yet. So maybe now's a good time to mention it is there's a hybrid version that you can do with with the VNL. So let's say you have, I'm just, this is just an example. Let's say you've got two and a half thousand words, three thousand words, there's a 3000 word idea you want to unpack. So one of the things you could do is you could put 500 words of that in in the VNL and it has all the context. It's it's it's the entire lead in. So, and then so then the other ball, in fact, they the entire single piece would be on the website minus all the little nuances and context that only makes sense inside of the VNL. They will be on the website. So there's this there's these two pieces. So now you've got people that are that are receiving your emails, and they're getting the value and the and the pool like this, this, this, this feel of tension, pulling them to one and discover this thing because it's it's interesting to them. And but now you're getting people to click. I think you did a PS of books, and I did a PS of podcasts that really resonated with me. So it was very much a mix. When we're creating I've had this thought many, many times like, I can't wait to write the year in review, and, and really to compile like everything we've done this year into some sort of coherent, like overview, it's it's incredible for me at least it's been incredibly inspiring and motivated. And, you know, we know, you know our plan, and we've said already a few times today that you know no plans to rise contact with reality. Like it's like, oh, okay, we, we have this book and year where we're going to get to go back and look and see. What have we done this year but what really have we done from the perspective of our newsletter, like what did we publish in our newsletter this year, and other things that we did and that I don't know why that's so inspiring to me, but it just it's such a wonderful way to bring one year to a close and I'm excited about the year that follows and we really we haven't really talked about, you know, how there are lots of things that we can do structurally in this there are lots of ways we can approach it there are lots of ways. We can have a PS and and add some texture to it, but for some reason that's been very inspiring and I think for people listening to this. Think about that as an option, even if you don't do it. I think it because you may get in the year like, oh, wow, I don't have the bandwidth for this, but keeping that in mind, it's a nice way to look back and see everything that you've accomplished. We've never talked about the year review idea that I'm curious to hear your take on how that fits into your thinking. So that's the idea of the of the year review. I've seen other people do versions where they'll do it in January. So they've, so they've kind of had their years done. They enjoyed their holiday and use the holiday to reflect back. And then in the January they essentially do their year, their previous year in review I've seen that happen for us we we use as a forcing mechanism to do it in the moment reflecting on the year before before any holidays. You know, there's there's just so many downstream benefits, some of which are obvious, some of which are not in doing something like a year and review. So when when when somebody gets to the end of an RBS. And that just goes a long way in reestablishing that relationship in in just a single click and then just looking at that stuff and it's a it's a rabbit hole it's an email full of rabbit holes and you know that's that's that's exactly what this world building things is is all about, but you know, creating doorways into these little rabbit holes that people get get lost in and and and before they know it they've consumed thousands and thousands of words of content or videos or podcasts or whatever, and that's that's essentially done a huge chunk of the relationship building stuff right there. I was just thinking in 2030 we can do a decade and review. You heard it here first. Alright, I'm going to transition to the second question. This is probably the question we've seen the most in the comments. So I'm looking forward to answering this one definitively. So, when it's not so much what's the difference let's think about in terms of when should someone use a hard bridge versus a soft bridge. Okay, there's clearly some we need some to do some work in the clarifying that but before we add that to the module. How should workshop participants think about the hard bridge versus the soft bridge. I think I'm going to put my neck on the line and say that every single bridge email should be a hard bridge email, except for one one use case, which is the soft and that's just wrapped up in in some context. I think every bridge email, unless I'm missing something here should really be a hard bridge because we are asking people to raise the hand for something that's that's going to happen after that. And we never want to presume that just because they've opted in at some point in time. We now have carte blanche over their attention and we, you know, and their permission to send them stuff forever. And so, I think every bridge email is a hard bridge email except for this one use case, which we can talk about. What do you think about that. That's a good question. I think of us. I guess I can think of a few scenarios, they're probably all the same scenario though, it's a hard bridge so, you know, I'm thinking when we did. We did the durable business series, it was, it was the fall fall enrollment fall 2020 enrollment for three courses for auto at the time auto responder madness, fear of influence in the traffic engine. We did a five part series that was about awareness engagement and conversion high level, no reference to our products. If I'm not mistaken, everybody got that. So, and that's and so that's correct. Yeah, so that to me would be like an opportunity to use a soft bridge and say hey, next week we're doing this five part series we think it's, we think it's valuable for everybody. And at the end of that five part series. And I mean we were transparent about it to be said that that that that five part series is half of a 10 part series, but that the next five parts the second five parts. We're going to specifically talk about how our courses fit into these ideas, and you have to raise your hand for that. So that was a soft bridge to hard bridge transition and that that to me. I could talk myself. I could talk myself into saying that we could, we could have used a hard bridge on the first one to certainly. But I think I'm okay with the fact that we obviously okay with it. I like that we there was a, there was a soft bridge just said hey this is coming up next week. And if someone chose to ignore it I don't know that we've. We've taken too much from them I think easy to delete. Yeah, so I could, you know, I can also, I could also imagine a soft bridge to me for me the way I think about the distinction between the soft bridge and a hard bridge is that a hard bridge separates trust from revenue. If there's revenue on the other side of a link. Then it's a hard break. Sorry, there's revenue on the other side of a of hand raising, then that's a hard bridge, because we were not going to show somebody an offer unless they said they're interested. So, information when the intent is to like, you know, let's assume that we put together a three part series. That's just generally useful. Like I like a soft bridge to say, hey, next week we're going to go on Monday, Wednesday, Friday, next week we're going to do this three part series on XYZ. We think you'll be interested in it. Just kind of like that. That's a me that a soft bridge there feels fine I feel like we've earned the right to do that with our audience. But I wouldn't do that if they're within those things there was a they could buy something if we were promoting something. So I don't know if that's in line with how you're thinking about it. I'm curious what your use case is that you're thinking about the soft bridge. Yeah, I agree. And I'm glad you you bought up that that use case. You know, because of what we were sending out, it was so in line with the broad, you know, the broad needs and, you know, why our subscribers are paying attention to us is the reason why we sent out that that series that was relevant to everybody. And if, if what we were saying in that thing was not relevant anymore, then those people, you know, could have unsubscribed, and we got unsubscribed, we definitely got unsubscribed. So in that case, I think it's fine and it's almost like a like an extension of a value newsletter that's that's themed. But again, just want to be be clear that because it's easy to do soft bridges doesn't mean it should be a soft bridge. Just because there's no revenue on the other side, I mean, sure, I'm at a good point and I love that that way of thinking of it, that if there is revenue, it needs to be a, at least from our perspective, it needs to be a hard bridge. But there's a million other versions where there's no revenue, but it's still should be a hard bridge. So if you've written a written a soap opera series. Most soap opera series should really be people raising their hand to get this thing. Not just because there's, well, there's no revenue in, in a certain soap opera series but because people raising their hand it's it's it's focusing their attention on this thing that's about to happen as opposed for us just to presume that the entire email list now has. We have the attention like 100% of them, which is not the case ever. So, you know, these hard bridges is just recognizing that at any point in time, we don't have the attention of every single person on our on our email on our email list, even if this thing is, is quite relevant for them. They might just be in some, some scenario that they just not going to pay attention and then just having those emails stack up in the inbox isn't a great thing so just be mindful of the fact that if you just think about it like a mental thing that well maybe every, every bridge should be a hard bridge unless and that unless needs to come with it with some, you know, just making sure that all the boxes are ticked and there's no reason why it shouldn't be a hard bridge and it can be a soft bridge where it's it's relevant for the broad majority of people in this moment. So I think yeah, I mean it's, it's kind of like a gray area but you know just just respect the fact that we don't have the attention of 100% of people on our email list and the bridges recognize the fact that that's the case and it's that's why we ask people and then they raise their hands and then it focuses their attention on this thing that's that's going to happen. And those people that didn't raise their hands for for something in this in this scenario maybe a soap opera series, they get to raise their hand next time. In fact, even for the same soap opera series that would be a second go at it. In the context of a, of a world that we built so just because they don't raise their hand to go into the same now they can go into some other time. So we shouldn't just presume that everybody's should get this thing. And it's, I always feel compelled to say, you know, there's nothing is absolute because they're, I keep thinking back to the, the one time we me violated the hard bridge rule so 2020 your birthday promotion we didn't have we, we sent an offer to everyone without a hard bridge right so I want, if someone's going to see that be like see that you know they do. But to, I want to at least talk about why we did that so that because a lot of thinking went into that so partly it was timing. And I'll just blame Andre for telling me late that it was his birthday or whatever but so the nature of that promotion. There was a really interesting story around it. And the story being that you know Andre sort of mentioned. You know it was birthday we usually do something and there are all these things we couldn't do. So I like a fool said I'll take care of it I'll come up with something and then there was in the process of creating an offer. I had to go back and really rely on and have conversations with others but really had to go back to the basics of what makes a good offer. So, if you, if you go back and you see that original promotion, yes it was sent to everybody know there was no hard bridge, but the critique I got from a lot of friends who are, you know, direct response marketers were like wow you really needed to get to the point here. And then I buried the offer at the end and it was like right because the, the main point of that email that we really wanted that Friday email particular was a newsletter we really wanted to deconstruct the offer creation process, and then the offer that was created, followed that process. So somebody the idea was somebody could read that email. Top to bottom have no interest whatsoever in the offer itself, but it was essentially a mini masterclass on offer creation, and then we gave everybody something for free that that was fit in on that Sunday, and it was kind of over Monday so we have, you know, we have, I don't want to say violated this rule we have. We have shown an offer to our entire audience at least once. But it was not. It wasn't something that we did. We can do this once in a while was very much like if we're going to do this, we have this tremendous responsibility to make sure it as it is as valuable as possible for everyone who's not at all interested that they could that they can save it for later. Come back and look at it think wow this is a this was this was really interesting and really useful independent of they didn't have to do anything to get value. It would be a further distinction right like we don't. The last thing we want to do is open the door to people you can get there to all these scenarios where you can just send everybody an offer that's not the point. But the point is that in rare circumstances, when, when there's so much value embedded in what you're doing that there's an exception to every rule or maybe that's just the rationalizing mistake, but we'll leave it at that. What should we do. One of the things we didn't discuss is the is the use case outside of what we've just you know all these gray areas that we've just spoken about is the ones scenario where we think that are, you know, it's always going to be okay. And that is, and we've we've drawn attention to this inside of the training anyway is if there's a product specific RBS. So let's just use auto email as an example somebody goes to the to the product page, and there's an opportunity for them to bar but they choose not to and they add themselves to the email sequence around out of email. Well, that's a signal to us that they interested but they're not ready to buy it. So that them opting in is the hard bridge is is is them essentially raising their hand in that moment that I want this thing. I want to know more about this thing. So in that case, in that scenario that RBS can transition. You know once it's done its thing, it can transition to a story power promotion, for example, that doesn't ask people to re raise the hand again because they've just done that. And you know you can't extend this, you know, infinity out and stick all sorts of little things in between because you know now it's now it's gone too long, but you know for them raising their hand about something that's very specific and it's around a product. Then, then that classes as a as a as a hard bridge them opting in, and then we can you can move straight from an RBS to a to a story power promotion. I think we think maybe we need a, yeah, maybe we need a third term. So maybe there's soft bridge, which is, you know, you're, you're going to a non like someone's going from something to something else that doesn't sell that transition is just letting them know, then there's a hard bridge that they have to raise their hand. And then this third scenario is like an embedded bridge, which is like, they've raised their hand at the beginning and said hey I'm interested in that thing. So we already know they're interested in the course whatever it is, and we, we emphasize the relationship first and then one of the emails transitions to, yeah, so let's just for the sake of argument say we have four emails that are there's it's it's talking about the thing in the, you know, it's establishing empathy about the need for the whatever the course whatever the history of it, how we came to create it. There's no link to sell it. Yeah, maybe the PS they can buy it directly or whatever but it's when we make that shift into why they would want to buy it, and what it will do for them and all of the things that are a typical story powered promotion that transition that the thing that goes from one to the other is like this. It's like an embedded bridge we're just kind of making that. Because it is it's critically important to draw attention to this fact that we don't, we're not doing this for the sake of making people jump through hoops we're, we're looking for the signal, and when someone says hey I want to know more about this course. It's a very strong signal that's a seven eight nine 10 above their head in that diagram right so they've already done that but we're still respecting them and we're still we're still focusing on the relationship and we're still having some kind of transition that's effectively this isn't how we would word it but effectively saying, you know, now, now that you kind of understand the background and sort of where this fits in a little bit start talking about how this can be helpful for you, specifically and then off we go with a story powered promotion. And that is a soft bridge. That's what it's, it's a soft bridge or an embedded bridge or whatever you want to call it. On the back of explicit permission to talk about the course there's no, we didn't trick anybody into thinking that this was not that we weren't going to be talking about a course. Yeah, I like that distinction. That's very valuable. Yeah, that's not that that's crystal clear, we can we can move on. We get 200 more questions. What's an embedded bridge. That's a perfect segue into moving on to the third topic which is story powered promotions. My maybe my fear about story powered promotions completely unfounded. And then you can unpack the implications and that's really the word I'm glad you drew attention to is often what we're doing is we're starting with something. And then we're unraveling all the implications of that something and that's a story. I mean, the so much of the art of email is based on the single observation, which is an observation of reality. Many people have made we refer to Dean Jackson about a lot but this has been made by infinite numbers of people who have looked at their data that most people who are going to buy from you are going to buy in the first 90 days, they're going to buy between day 91 and two years like that's, that's reality that's the equivalent of gravity in our world, and that in and of itself. It's interesting like, oh wow but the art of email, really everything that follows the course is is a byproduct of the implications of that reality. And you can create an entire course on implications of a single observation, a single data point so there's absolutely no reason that when you find some something that's interesting to you that peaks your curiosity.
